elements.sent-2411-001: Advanced Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with recent Knowledge-based VQA tasks, such as INFOSEEK and Encyclopedic-VQA, due to their limited and frozen knowledge scope, often leading to ambiguous and inaccurate responses.
elements.sent-2411-002: Thus, multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (mRAG) is naturally introduced to provide MLLMs with comprehensive and up-to-date knowledge, effectively expanding the knowledge scope.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, current mRAG methods have inherent drawbacks, including: 1) Performing retrieval even when external knowledge is not needed.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these shortcomings, we propose a novel generalized framework called multimodal Retrieval-Reflection-Augmented Generation (mR$^2$AG), which achieves adaptive retrieval and useful information localization to enable answers through two easy-to-implement reflection operations, preventing high model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-005: In mR$^2$AG, Retrieval-Reflection is designed to distinguish different user queries and avoids redundant retrieval calls, and Relevance-Reflection is introduced to guide the MLLM in locating beneficial evidence of the retrieved content and generating answers accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-006: In addition, mR$^2$AG can be integrated into any well-trained MLLM with efficient fine-tuning on the proposed mR$^2$AG Instruction-Tuning dataset (mR$^2$AG-IT).
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{fig/fig1.pdf} \caption{ Comparisons of different methods on Visual-dependent and Knowledge-based VQA tasks: 1) Typical MLLMs use the image $I$ and question $Q$ as inputs, offering limited support for Knowledge-based questions. . 2) Naive mRAG use $I$, $Q$, and retrieved content $P_{1,2,3}$ as inputs in all cases, inevitably introducing irrelevant noise. 3) mR$^2$AG adaptively determines the necessity of retrieval and effectively locates the useful context, \ie, $P_3$ for $Q_2$. } \label{fig:1}
elements.sent-2411-007: The rapid development of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) \cite{alayrac2022flamingo, li2023blip, liu2024visual, liu2023improved, achiam2023gpt, reid2024gemini enables them to excel in Visual-dependent VQA tasks that rely solely on visual content or common sense, such as VQAv2 \cite{goyal2017making , GQA \cite{hudson2019gqa and TextVQA \cite{singh2019towards .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, recently proposed Knowledge-based VQA tasks like INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can and Encyclopedic-VQA \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic , introduce fine-grained visual entities and focus on encyclopedic knowledge, posing significant new challenges for existing MLLMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: As shown in $Q_2$ of Figure.~\ref{fig:1 , when queried about the first flight date of an airplane, typical MLLMs tend to provide inaccurate and overly general responses due to their limited and frozen knowledge scope.}
elements.sent-2411-010: To obtain accurate and specific answers, some works \cite{hu2024avis, caffagni2024wiki, yan2024echosight introduce multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (mRAG) that leverages external knowledge bases.}
elements.sent-2411-011: They first use retrievers to find query-related information, then input them into the MLLM for answer generation.
elements.sent-2411-012: Nevertheless, several challenges remain: 1) Indiscriminate use of retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-013: Visual-dependent questions usually do not require external knowledge, and conducting retrieval for them may introduce noise and lead to wrong answers, such as the response of Naive mRAG to $Q_1$ in Figure \ref{fig:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: When encountering questions beyond one's knowledge, humans first search for relevant information and then obtain reliable answers by locating direct evidence, \eg, $P_3$ of $P$ in Figure \ref{fig:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, current methods input retrieved text into the model to directly generate answers, lacking an explicit evidence localization process, making it difficult to determine whether the model can effectively utilize the useful retrieved information.
elements.sent-2411-016: To improve performance, some methods introduce complex rules or even external models to calculate query-passage correlations to filter retrieved content.
elements.sent-2411-017: To address the above challenges, we propose a novel generalized framework called multimodal Retrieval-Reflection-Augmented Generation (mR$^2$AG), designed to seamlessly leverage the inherent instruction-following, multimodal understanding and reasoning abilities in MLLMs to enhance Knowledge-based question answering.
elements.sent-2411-018: For clarifying the necessity of retrieval, mR$^2$AG introduces Retrieval-Reflection to determine whether the user's query is Knowledge-based or Visual-dependent for adaptive retrieval, thus expanding MLLMs' knowledge scope while maintaining the original performance.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, mR$^2$AG imitates human behavior to implement Relevance-Reflection, which guides the model to explicitly assess the evidence parts in all retrieved information, and generates accurate responses based on the identified evidence.
elements.sent-2411-020: The implementation of the proposed reflection operations involves only modifications to the MLLMs' vocabulary, without introducing any additional modules or computational strategies to destroy the original structure of the models, thereby effectively coupling with the MLLMs' existing capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-021: To quickly integrate mR$^2$AG into pre-trained MLLMs, we provide a corresponding mR$^2$AG Instruction Tuning dataset (mR$^2$AG-IT) specifically constructed for Knowledge-based VQA tasks through an automated annotation pipeline..
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, mR$^2$AG-IT annotates the evidence paragraphs within Wikipedia articles that explicitly support user queries.
elements.sent-2411-023: Comprehensive comparisons on Knowledge-based VQA datasets show that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-024: When using LLaVA-v1.5-7B \cite{liu2024visual as the base MLLM, applying mR$^2$AG not only achieves performance gains of 10.6\% and 15.5\% over the previous SOTAs on the INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$ and INFOSEEK$_{\text{Wikidata }$ test sets, but also surpasses SOTAs on the Encyclopedic-VQA (Enc-VQA) test set by 2.5\% on single-hop questions and 18.2\% on multi-answer questions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, our method retains the excellent capabilities of the base MLLM on Visual-dependent VQA tasks, demonstrating performance comparable to LLaVA-v1.5-7B across various benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-026: The contributions of this work are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-027: We propose an advanced multimodal RAG framework mR$^2$AG, which only uses two reflection operations to stimulate MLLMs to implement retrieval invocation, evidence content identification, and answer generation.
elements.sent-2411-028: We provide the mR$^2$AG-IT dataset, which aims to quickly adapt MLLMs to Knowledge-based VQA and serves as a supplement to general visual instruction tuning datasets.
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, mR$^2$AG exhibits conciseness and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-030: Several works \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic, chen2023can focus on Knowledge-based VQA and construct corresponding datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Early OK-VQA \cite{marino2019ok and its variant A-OKVQA \cite{schwenk2022okvqa emphasize the significance of knowledge in VQA but primarily focus on commonsense.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: ViQuAE \cite{lerner2022viquae introduces a wide range of entity types and tests fine-grained knowledge related to named entities.}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, certain questions in ViQuAE can be answered without viewing the visual content.
elements.sent-2411-034: INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can and Enc-VQA \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic address this by designing questions that force the model to examine the image for the correct answer.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: These datasets cover a broad range of Wikipedia entities and focus on fine-grained knowledge related to these entities.
elements.sent-2411-036: In contrast, INFOSEEK explicitly defines two splits: Unseen Entity and unseen Question, whereas Enc-VQA introduces more diverse question types, including single-hop, multi-answer, and two-hop questions.
elements.sent-2411-038: The rapid development of LLMs \cite{brown2020language, ouyang2022training, chowdhery2023palm, touvron2023llama, chiang2023vicuna, chung2024scaling drives the progress of MLLMs \cite{alayrac2022flamingo, li2023blip, liu2024visual, liu2023improved, achiam2023gpt, reid2024gemini, zhu2024mipha .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Typical MLLMs, \eg, LLaVA, widely adopt a two-stage pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm, achieving impressive capabilities across various multimodal tasks \cite{singh2019towards, li2023seed, yue2023mmmu, li2023evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-040: MLLMs perform well in understanding human queries \cite{liu2024visual , handling purely visual VQA tasks \cite{goyal2017making, hudson2019gqa , and addressing commonsense VQA tasks \cite{marino2019ok .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, they struggle with Knowledge-based VQA tasks that involve fine-grained knowledge of specific visual entities, as shown by their performance on INFOSEEK and Enc-VQA \cite{chen2023can, mensink2023encyclopedic .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/fig2.pdf} \caption{ Overview of the mR$^2$AG framework. (a1) mR$^2$AG w/ Retrieval: This process includes: a) Retrieval-Reflection for determining the necessity of retrieval; b) Relevance-Reflection for identifying evidence passages; c) Post-processing multiple potential answers. (a2) mR$^2$AG w/o Retrieval: The generation process when retrieval is unnecessary. (b) Naïve mRAG: A baseline method without reflection. } \label{fig:2}
elements.sent-2411-042: RAG is widely used in LLMs to address challenges like hallucinations \cite{zhang2023siren , non-renewable knowledge, and opaque reasoning \cite{kandpal2023large, gao2023retrieval .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: It combines LLMs' inherent knowledge with dynamic external knowledge, offering a solution for knowledge-intensive tasks \cite{lewis2020retrieval, mao2020generation .}
elements.sent-2411-044: This technique is gaining traction within the MLLMs domain.
elements.sent-2411-045: For example, Wiki-LLaVA \cite{caffagni2024wiki retrieves Wikipedia articles from the input image and uses Contriever \cite{gautier2022unsupervised to select relevant passages.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: EchoSight \cite{yan2024echosight introduces a fine-tuned Q-Former \cite{li2023blip architecture as a reranker, filtering the retrieved content based on both the input image and question.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: These methods rely on external models to filter the retrieved information, while overlooking the role of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-048: Inspired by SELF-RAG \cite{asai2023self , we propose the mR$^2$AG framework, which leverages MLLMs to independently localize the query-relevant evidence within the retrieved content, eliminating the need for additional modules or complex strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Knowledge-based VQA takes an image-question pair $(I, Q)$ as input and is supported by an accessible knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-050: As shown in Figure.~\ref{fig:2 , naive mRAG first retrieves the top-$N$ articles most relevant to the input from the knowledge base, denoted as $\hat{P = \{\hat{P_i}\}_{i=1 ^{N}$, and then feeds the concatenation of $\hat{P}$ and the $(I, Q)$ into the MLLM to directly generate the response $y^{\mathrm{ans }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In contrast, mR$^2$AG proposes two novel reflection operations to decouple the generation process into three steps: (1) Performing Retrieval-Reflection to determine whether retrieval is needed.
elements.sent-2411-052: The presentation of mR$^2$AG is organized as follows: Section \ref{sec:generation introduces the mR$^2$AG method.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Section \ref{sec:training describes the training of mR$^2$AG.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Section \ref{sec:dataset introduces the mR$^2$AG-IT dataset for fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-055: User queries can be divided into Visual-dependent and Knowledge-based according to the input $(I, Q)$.
elements.sent-2411-056: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:2 , the question in case $(a1)$ requires external knowledge for a confident answer, while the question in case $(a2)$ can be answered entirely relying on visual content.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Introducing external knowledge in the latter case may bring undesirable noise.
elements.sent-2411-058: To guide the model in distinguishing between different queries, we define two special tokens: [Retrieval]/[No Retrieval], to perform Retrieval-Reflection.
elements.sent-2411-059: First, the model generates retrieval-reflection predictions $y^{\mathrm{ret }$ based on the input $(I, Q)$: \begin{equation}y^{\mathrm{ret}} = MLLM(I,Q).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Depending on the different results of $y^{\mathrm{ret }$, one of the following ways is executed:.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We use English Wikipedia entries as the knowledge base, where the $k_{th}$ entry consists of a candidate image $\hat{I_k}$, title $\hat{T_k}$, and article $\hat{P_k}$.
elements.sent-2411-062: CLIP \cite{radford2021learning is utilized to encode $I$, $\hat{I_k}$ and $\hat{T_k}$, and calculates the cosine similarity of $\mathrm{sim (I, \hat{I_k )$ and $\mathrm{sim (I, \hat{T_k )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The overall retrieval score $S^{\mathrm{ret _k$ for the $k_{th}$ entry is the average of two cosine similarities: \begin{equation}\label{score:sim} S^{\mathrm{ret}}_k = \left( \mathrm{sim}(I, \hat{I_k}) + \mathrm{sim}(I, \hat{T_k}) \right) /2.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The supposed result $\hat{P = \{\hat{P_i}\}_{i=1 ^{N}$ correspond to the articles of the top-$N$ entries with the highest retrieval scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: We divide each retrieved article $\hat{P _{i}$ into multiple natural paragraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-066: To enable the model to determine whether each segmented paragraph $\hat{P _{ij}$ contains evidence relevant to the question $Q$, we introduce two relevance-reflection tokens: [Relevant]/[Irrelevant].}
elements.sent-2411-067: The model conditioned on the combination of $\hat{P _{ij}$ and the query $(I, Q)$ generates the relevance-reflection prediction $y^{\mathrm{rel _{ij}$: \begin{equation}\label{score: rel} y^{\mathrm{rel}}_{ij} = \mathrm{MLLM}(I, Q, \hat{P}_{ij}).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: According to the result of $y^{\mathrm{rel _{ij}$, mR$^2$AG selects one of the following processes to perform:.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Multiple evidence passages may exist in an article, leading to generate multiple candidate answers.
elements.sent-2411-070: Therefore, post-processing is necessary to arrive at a single final answer.
elements.sent-2411-071: Based on the retrieval-reflection-augmented generation process, we apply a hierarchical post-processing to rank the candidate answers by integrating scores at three levels:.
elements.sent-2411-072: The retrieval score in Equation.~\ref{score:sim measures the similarity between the query image $I$ and the candidate Wikipedia entry, which serves as the Retrieval-Reflection score $S^{\mathrm{ret _{i}$ for the $i_{th}$ retrieved entry.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The probability of generating the \([Relevant]\) Relevance-Reflection token quantifies the model's confidence in judging $\hat{P _{ij}$ as evidence, which can be defined as the Relevance-Reflection score \(S^{\mathrm{rel _{ij}\): \begin{equation}S^{\mathrm{rel}}_{ij} = p_{\theta}\left( y^{\mathrm{rel}}_{ij} = [Relevant] \mid I, Q, \hat{P}_{ij} \right),\end{equation} where \(\theta\) represents the parameters of MLLM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: We calculate the probability of each token in the generated answer sequence and use the geometric mean to normalize the influence of sequence length variation, resulting in the answer confidence score \(S^{\mathrm{ans _{ij}\): \begin{equation}\label{score:ans} S^{\mathrm{ans}}_{ij} = \sqrt[n]{p_{\theta}(y^{ans}_{ij})},\end{equation} where \(n\) represents the sequence length.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: This score reflects the model's confidence in generating answers based on the retrieved content and reflection tokens.
elements.sent-2411-076: The three levels of scores comprehensively consider each step in the answer generation process, evaluating the reliability of the candidate answers at the entry, passage, and answer levels, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-077: The effects of the three scores are integrated by calculating their product, which serves as the final criterion for ranking candidate answers.
elements.sent-2411-078: The model outputs the answer with the highest score based on this criterion.
elements.sent-2411-079: During instruction tuning, we combine the common visual instruction tuning dataset, such as LLaVA-IT \cite{liu2024visual , with the specifically designed mR$^2$AG-IT dataset:.}
elements.sent-2411-080: For each sample in LLaVA-IT, we set the Retrieval-Reflection token to [No Retrieval].
elements.sent-2411-081: The model is trained to answer the questions depends only on the visual content, and the training loss is formulated as: \begin{equation}L_1 = -E_{(I,Q,y^{ret},y^{ans}) \sim D_{LLaVA-IT}} \log p_{\theta}(y^{ret}= [No Retrieval], y^{ans} \mid I,Q). \notag\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: For each sample in mR$^2$AG-IT, we set the Retrieval-Reflection token to [Retrieval].
elements.sent-2411-083: The model is trained to invoke retrievers, identify evidence passages, and generate accurate response.
elements.sent-2411-084:
The training loss can be defined as:  egin{equation}L_2 = -E_{(I,Q,y^{ret},\hat{P}_{ij},y^{rel}_{ij},y^{ans}) \sim D_{mR$^2$AG-IT}} \Big( \log p_{	heta}(y^{ret}=[Retrieval] |I,Q) 
otag + \mathbbm{1} ig[y^{rel}_{ij}=[Relevant] ig] \log p_{	heta}(y^{rel}_{ij}, y^{ans} |I,Q,\hat{P}_{ij}) 
otag + \mathbbm{1} ig[y^{rel}_{ij}=[Irrelevant] ig] \log p_{	heta}(y^{rel}_{ij} |I,Q,\hat{P}_{ij})\Big), 
otag\end{equation} where the indicator function $\mathbbm{1 [\cdot]$ equals to 1 when the condition inside the parentheses is satisfied and 0 otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Each sample in these training datasets includes a \((I, Q, P_i, A)\) quadruple, where \(P_i\) is the ground truth Wikipedia article containing the answer source.
elements.sent-2411-086: For INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can , the annotation process includes:.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The Wikipedia article is segmented into natural paragraphs, with each paragraph \(P_{ij}\) preserving semantic independence and forming a new quadruple \((V, Q, P_{ij , A)\).}
elements.sent-2411-088: Each paragraph \(P_{ij}\) is evaluated by GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt with the question \(Q\) and answer \(A\) to determine if it serves as evidence.}
elements.sent-2411-089: If \(P_{ij}\) is relevant to the query and supports answer generation, the Relevance-Reflection token is labeled as $y^{rel _{ij}$ = [Relevant]; otherwise, $y^{rel _{ij}$ = [Irrelevant].}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Due to the limited quantity of Wikipedia articles in the INFOSEEK training set, we additionally incorporate the Natural Questions (NQ) dataset \cite{kwiatkowski2019natural as a supplement.}
elements.sent-2411-091: This dataset comprises of real queries from the Google search engine, each with a long answer (when available) and a short answer as the response to the query.
elements.sent-2411-092: We consider the long answer as evidence for the query and the short answer as the response.
elements.sent-2411-093: For Enc-VQA \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic , each sample is annotated with the evidence paragraphs from the ground truth Wikipedia article \(P_i\) that support the answer, eliminating additional steps to identify the evidence paragraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-094: To ensure the precision of all training samples, we conduct strict string searches for filtering, ensuring answers appear only in evidence paragraphs.

elements.sent-2411-095: INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can contains a training set and three evaluation sets: INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation }$, INFOSEEK$_{\text{Wikidata }$ and INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The training set, along with INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation }$ and INFOSEEK$_{\text{Wikidata }$, are all derived from 1.3M samples automatically constructed from Wikipedia to support large-scale training and evaluation.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$ consists of 8.9K samples annotated by humans to simulate real information-seeking intentions.}
elements.sent-2411-098: To prevent overfitting, each evaluation set is divided into two subsets: Unseen Entity and Unseen Question.
elements.sent-2411-099: The evaluation samples can be divided into three categories, \ie, STRING, TIME, and NUMERICAL.
elements.sent-2411-100: The STRING and TIME categories use VQA Accuracy \cite{goyal2017making as the evaluation metric, while the NUMERICAL category is assessed with Relaxed Accuracy \cite{methani2020plotqa .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: To calculate the overall accuracy, the average score for each question is first computed separately for each test split, followed by the geometric mean of these averages.
elements.sent-2411-102: INFOSEEK uses a Wikipedia knowledge base containing 100K articles and infobox images as external knowledge sources for the With-KB protocol.
elements.sent-2411-103: Since this external knowledge base is not publicly available, we construct one of the same scale and perform comprehensive evaluations across all available datasets.
elements.sent-2411-104: The evaluation metrics strictly follow INFOSEEK's standards, ensuring a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-105: Encyclopedic VQA \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic contains 1M \{I, Q, A\} triples, covering 16.7K entities and totaling 221K unique Q+A pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Each Q+A pair is associated with up to 5 images, showing various instances of the same entity.
elements.sent-2411-107: The dataset includes single-hop questions generated using templated and automatic methods, along with multi-answer and two-hop questions.
elements.sent-2411-108: Multi-answer questions require a list of possible answers, while two-hop questions involve two consecutive retrieval and reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-109: For evaluation, accuracy is measured as the percentage of matches between the predicted and ground-truth answers on the test split, using the BERT Matching (BEM) \cite{bulian2022tomayto standard for correctness assessment.}
elements.sent-2411-110: For multi-answer questions, the model's output is first converted into a set of strings, and the intersection-over-the-union (IoU) with the ground-truth set is calculated.
elements.sent-2411-111: If $\text{IoU \geq 0.5$, the prediction is considered correct; otherwise, BEM is used to determine the equivalence between the predicted and ground-truth lists.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Enc-VQA provides a controlled knowledge base comprising 2M Wikipedia pages, with each Q+A pair annotated with the corresponding Wikipedia articles and evidence paragraphs supporting the answers.
elements.sent-2411-113: In the retrieval-augmented setting, Enc-VQA employs a Google Lens-based \cite{google_lens retriever to predict entities from the input image $I$.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We focus on single-hop and multi-answer questions in Enc-VQA, evaluating on the test split with a consistent knowledge base, retrieval approach and unified evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-115: We perform instruction tuning using the combination of the LLaVA instruction tuning dataset (LLaVA-IT) \cite{liu2024visual and the mR$^2$AG-IT dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Training continues from the stage-1 checkpoint of the LLaVA-v1.5-7B, with a learning rate of \(2 \times 10^{-5}\) and a batch size of \(8 \times 16\), and lasts for one epoch.
elements.sent-2411-117: The main experiments and ablation studies are conducted based on the LLaVA-v1.5-7B \cite{liu2024visual , while mR$^2$AG is also applicable to other MLLMs, such as Mini-Gemini \cite{li2024mini and Mipha \cite{zhu2024mipha .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: We use CLIP-ViT-L/14@336px \cite{radford2021learning as the retriever for INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can , and directly use the retrieval results based on Google Lens for Enc-VQA \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: By default, we utilize the top-5 Wikipedia entries from the retrieval results for both benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-120: In the setting without external knowledge, the model predicts the answer based solely on the input image and question, relying on the knowledge stored in its parameters during training.
elements.sent-2411-121: To explore the performance of MLLMs under this protocol, we fine-tune LLaVA-v1.5-7B \cite{liu2024visual using \{V, Q, A\} triples from the INFOSEEK training set.}
elements.sent-2411-122: After fine-tuning, the model's performance on INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$ improves from 9.5 to 12.0, and on INFOSEEK$_{\text{Wikidata }$ from 9.1 to 20.5.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Additionally, the strongest models, GPT-4v \cite{achiam2023gpt and GPT-4o \cite{gpt4o2024 , achieve scores of 12.1 and 21.3 on INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: These results suggest that while fine-tuning for specific datasets or using stronger models helps improve performance, current models still fall short in knowledge-based VQA tasks, underscoring the need for external knowledge.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Main results of various methods on Enc-VQA\cite{mensink2023encyclopedic}. By default, these methods use Google Lens \cite{google_lens} as the retriever, while methods marked with $^*$ use a custom retrieval scheme.} \label{tab:enc} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Retrieved} Oracle Single-hop Multi-answer Singel-hop PaLI \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic} 28.1\% 9.2\% 48.8\% PaLM \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic} 48.8\% 33.6\% 87.0\% GPT-3 \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic} 44.9\% 32.1\% 82.1\% Wiki-LLaVA$^*$ \cite{caffagni2024wiki} 21.8\% -- 39.2\% EchoSight$^*$ \cite{yan2024echosight} 41.8\% -- -- HAMMR-BLIP-2 \cite{castrejon2024hammr} 45.0\% -- -- HAMMR-PaLI-X \cite{castrejon2024hammr} 47.8\% -- -- Cascade w/o vanilla MLLMs \cite{alazraki2023not} 53.4\% -- -- \rowcolor{mygray} LLaVA-mR$^2$AG 55.9\% 51.8\% 88.2\% \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-125: Table.~\ref{tab:infoseek presents the comparisons of models utilizing an external knowledge base on the INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can benchmark.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: When leveraging the articles of the retrieved Wikipedia entries for answer generation, our mR$^2$AG significantly outperforms the best existing models on all three test sets.
elements.sent-2411-127: Specifically, mR$^2$AG surpasses LLM-RA \cite{jian2024large , CLIP→FiD \cite{chen2023can , and EchoSight \cite{yan2024echosight by 15.5\%, 10.6\%, and 8.9\% in overall accuracy on INFOSEEK$_{\text{Wikidata }$, INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$, and INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: To further verify that our improvement stems from the proposed mR$^2$AG framework rather than simply the improved retrieval method or the powerful MLLM, we implement two comparative models: LLaVA-mRAG and LLaVA-SFR.
elements.sent-2411-129: Both models use the same retrieval results as ours.
elements.sent-2411-130: LLaVA-mRAG directly uses the articles of the retrieved entries to augment generation, while LLaVA-SFR employs an off-the-shelf model, SFR \cite{meng2024sfrembedding , to identify question-related paragraphs in the articles for augmenting generation.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Our results significantly outperform these two models, demonstrating that our method can effectively utilize noisy retrieval content, accurately pinpoint the relevant information, and extract the knowledge needed to answer the questions.. Oracle Knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-132: Even when it is possible to obtain the ground-truth Wikipedia entries corresponding to the involved entities in the question, and use them to augment the answer generation, our method still surpasses other methods across all test sets.
elements.sent-2411-133: This further highlights the advantage of mR$^2$AG in refining useful evidence, and explicitly outputting evaluation results before generating answers improves the model's ability to perform knowledge reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-134: As Table.~\ref{tab:enc illustrates, when using the retrieved knowledge, mR$^2$AG achieves superior performance, improving from 53.4\% to 55.9\% on single-hop questions and significantly increasing from 33.6\% to 51.8\% on the overlooked multi-answer questions.}
elements.sent-2411-135: This indicates that mR$^2$AG can effectively analyze noisy retrieved knowledge, accurately locate relevant information, and generate high-quality responses.
elements.sent-2411-136: Under the Oracle setting, when provided with more reliable knowledge sources, the performance of mR$^2$AG improves dramatically from 55.9\% to 88.2\%, surpassing the previous SOTA of 87.0\%.
elements.sent-2411-137: This suggests that: 1) Improving retrieval precision effectively enhances the performance of mR$^2$AG, demonstrating the method’s high potential.
elements.sent-2411-138: Overall, the state-of-the-art performance across multiple Knowledge-based VQA tasks showcases the broad applicability of mR$^2$AG.

elements.sent-2411-139: We conduct comprehensive evaluations on widely used Visual-dependent benchmarks to demonstrate mR$^2$AG's capabilities in handling questions about visual content or common sense.
elements.sent-2411-140: As shown in Table.~\ref{tab:mllm_bench , our model performs comparably to the base MLLM, \ie, LLaVA-V1.5-7B \cite{liu2023improved , on the MME \cite{fu2024mme benchmark and surpasses it on LLaVA\textsuperscript{W \cite{liu2023improved , MMB \cite{liu2024mmbench , and POPE \cite{li2023evaluating benchmarks.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: These comparisons highlight the effectiveness of the mR$^2$AG design, which strategically separates Visual-dependent tasks from Knowledge-based VQA tasks, allowing for targeted optimization in Knowledge-based domains while maintaining excellent performance on Visual-dependent tasks.
elements.sent-2411-142: We validate the effectiveness of the mR$^2$AG framework on three model architectures (Mipha \cite{zhu2024mipha , Mini-Gemini \cite{li2024mini , and LLaVA \cite{liu2024visual ), covering three different scales of language models (3B, 7B, and 13B).}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: In our experiments, we fine-tune these MLLMs from their stage-1 checkpoints using the same instruction-tuning data, while maintaining consistent hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size and number of epochs.
elements.sent-2411-144: The results in Table.~\ref{tab:gen_sca show that these base models generally perform poorly on the INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can benchmark.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: However, by introducing the naive mRAG approach or the mR$^2$AG framework, we observe significant improvements in performance on Knowledge-based VQA tasks.
elements.sent-2411-146: Notably, the mR$^2$AG framework consistently outperforms the naive mRAG approach, demonstrating its generalizability across different model architectures and scalability across varying language model sizes.

elements.sent-2411-147: In this section, we conduct ablation studies to verify the effectiveness of our model's design choices.
elements.sent-2411-148: Most comparisons are performed on the INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation }$ dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-150: To evaluate the effectiveness of using the product of the Retrieval-Reflection score $S^{\mathrm{ret }$, Relevance-Reflection score $S^{\mathrm{rel }$, and answer confidence score $S^{\mathrm{ans }$ in post-processing, we conducted a comprehensive comparison with all alternative ranking methods, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:rank .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: As observed, randomly selecting from answer candidates yields the lowest performance, which is not desirable.
elements.sent-2411-152: When using scores for post-processing, the combination of all three scores is superior to other score combinations, demonstrating that these scores effectively assess the reliability of the answer at different levels.
elements.sent-2411-153: Effect of retrieving different numbers of Wikipedia entries.
elements.sent-2411-154: To provide insights into the optimal number of retrieved Wikipedia entries for augmenting generation, we explore how varying the number of entries affects performance, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:topk .}
elements.sent-2411-155: Increasing the number of retrieved entries from 1 to 5 significantly improves the overall accuracy of INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation }$, as higher recall rates increase the likelihood of capturing relevant information.}
elements.sent-2411-156: However, further increasing the number of retrieved entries introduces more irrelevant content and noise, leading to limited performance improvement and higher inference costs.
elements.sent-2411-157: Considering the trade-off between performance and efficiency, we select 5 retrieved Wikipedia entries for each question.
elements.sent-2411-158: Contribution of the NQ samples to the mR$^2$AG-IT dataset.
elements.sent-2411-159: NQ samples serve as a supplement to the mR$^2$AG-IT dataset, which contains samples with evidence paragraphs.
elements.sent-2411-160: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:w/o_nq , removing this portion of data leads to a performance decline.}
elements.sent-2411-161: This finding highlights the importance of including NQ data in enhancing the model's ability to accurately identify evidence paragraphs.
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{The importance of NQ dataset.} \label{tab:w/o_nq} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l>{ \arraybackslash}p{1.2cm}>{ \arraybackslash}p{1.0cm}>{ \arraybackslash}p{0.8cm}} \multicolumn{3}{c}{INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation}}$} Unseen Question Unseen Entity Overall w/o NQ 39.1 39.7 39.4 w/ NQ 40.6 39.8 40.2 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-162: Benefits of combining cross-modal and uni-modal retrievals.
elements.sent-2411-163: Table~\ref{tab:entity_recall compares the performance of different methods for retrieving the ground-truth Wikipedia entities on INFOSEEK$_{\text{Validation }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: It is evident that combining cross-modal and uni-modal retrievals significantly improves R@1/10/20, thereby incorporating more knowledge beneficial for answering the questions.
elements.sent-2411-165: In Figure.~\ref{fig:3 , we present the qualitative comparison of the mR$^2$AG with GPT-4o.}
elements.sent-2411-166: As illustrated in Figure.~\ref{fig:3 (a) and (b), our method retrieves relevant knowledge and accurately answers the Knowledge-based questions, achieving more precise results compared to GPT-4o.}
elements.sent-2411-167: However, two failure cases are shown in Figure.\ref{fig:3 :.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Inaccurate retrieval: As illustrated in Figure.~\ref{fig:3 (c), when the subject in the image is difficult to identify, the retriever struggles to find relevant information, making it challenging for our method to answer the questions.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Knowledge interference: In Figure.~\ref{fig:3 (d), the retriever finds the correct entity, but our method provides the wrong answer due to the conflicting knowledge in the text, specifically ''a suspended span of 564 feet.''.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{Comparisons of retrieval performance across different retrieval methods on the INFOSEEK knowledge base.} \label{tab:entity_recall} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l>{ \arraybackslash}p{0.6cm}>{ \arraybackslash}p{0.6cm}>{ \arraybackslash}p{0.6cm}} Retrieval Method R@1 R@10 R@20 Cross-modal 0.31 0.58 0.65 Uni-modal 0.29 0.55 0.62 Cross-modal + Uni-modal 0.38 0.65 0.71 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-170: This paper proposes an advanced multimodal RAG framework that optimizes Knowledge-based VQA tasks while maintaining the model's capabilities as a general-purpose MLLM.
elements.sent-2411-171: The approach is based on existing MLLMs, guiding the model to explicitly distinguish the type of user query and evaluate retrieved information to refine the naive multimodal RAG process.
elements.sent-2411-172: It improves inference efficiency by adaptively avoiding unnecessary retrievals.
elements.sent-2411-173: Furthermore, by explicitly evaluating the retrieved content, it can identify the evidence passages relevant to the query, while filtering out noise from the retrieved content, thereby enhancing the credibility of the generated responses.
elements.sent-2411-174: Future work will explore knowledge graph-based retrieval-augmented systems and broader application scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-175: Limited by the entity recognition capabilities of existing MLLMs, our method is quite dependent on the retriever, assuming that the default visual entity occupies the major position in the image.
elements.sent-2411-176: In the future, we plan to conduct specialized training for visual entity recognition tasks and guide the model to enhance the discrimination of visual entity recognition categories and locations.
elements.sent-2411-177: Inspired by the chain-of-thought \cite{wei2022chain approach, the prompt instructs GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt to extract evidence sentences before generating relevance judgments.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: This design not only enhances the accuracy of the judgments but also facilitates manual calibration.
elements.sent-2411-179: For each input, the content within curly braces \{\} is replaced with the corresponding actual input.
elements.sent-2411-180: In the following, ''question'', ''answer'', and ''paragraph'' correspond to $Q$, $A$, and $P_{ij}$, respectively, as defined in Section~\ref{sec:dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-181: If so, please respond with [Relevant] and find the evidence sentence supporting the answer.
elements.sent-2411-182: If not, please just respond with [Irrelevant].
elements.sent-2411-183: There are only two formats for your response:.
elements.sent-2411-186: INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can evaluates generated answers using exact match, requiring the outputs to strictly match the annotated answers, which are typically concise and presented in the form of a single word or phrase.}
elements.sent-2411-187: To ensure the outputs align with these requirements, we design the following prompt, guiding the model to focus on retrieved content and produce concise responses: ''Based on the retrieved document, answer the question with a single word or phrase.''.
elements.sent-2411-188: The Enc-VQA \cite{mensink2023encyclopedic dataset includes both single-hop and multi-answer questions.}
elements.sent-2411-189: For single-hop questions, we adopt the same prompt template as INFOSEEK.
elements.sent-2411-190: For multiple-answer questions, the model needs to generate several possible answers.
elements.sent-2411-191: We adjust the prompt to ensure the answers comply with the dataset requirements, enabling effective extraction of answer lists from the responses for evaluation: ''Based on the retrieved documents, answer the question as briefly as possible, using '\&\&' to connect multiple different answers.''.
elements.sent-2411-192: Tables~\ref{table:human_question_type , \ref{table:val_question_type , and \ref{table:test_question_type present the complete experimental results on INFOSEEK \cite{chen2023can across various question types.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: In the without external knowledge setting, the model relies solely on the knowledge encoded in its parameters to answer questions.
elements.sent-2411-194: As shown in Tables~\ref{table:human_question_type and \ref{table:test_question_type , we fine-tune the LLaVA model without integrating external knowledge.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: The results indicate that this approach only leads to limited performance improvements.
elements.sent-2411-196: Additionally, using APIs, we evaluate the performance of GPT-4v/o \cite{achiam2023gpt, gpt4o2024 and Gemini-1.5-pro \cite{reid2024gemini on INFOSEEK$_{\text{Human }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: As shown in Table~\ref{table:human_question_type , although the GPT series models outperform other fine-tuned models, they remain inferior to the mR$^2$AG framework.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Overall, the complete experimental results on the INFOSEEK dataset demonstrate that mR$^2$AG significantly improves accuracy across all question types, with the most notable enhancement observed in the Time category.
elements.sent-2411-199: These findings further underscore the superiority of our approach in addressing Knowledge-based VQA tasks.
